text: The surgeon begins creating a flap in the surgical field, preparing for further dissection in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon is dissecting in the retrorectus space medial to the semilunar line, carefully separating tissue layers in this area.
The surgeon inadvertently drops the epigastric vessels and proceeds to sweep them back up into position.
The surgeon reaches the semilunar line above the arcuate line and performs a limited release of the transversus abdominis muscle to access the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon points out the area below the arcuate line, indicating the anatomical location relevant to the ongoing procedure.
The surgeon identifies the arcuate line at the level of the Spigelian hernia, highlighting its anatomical location.
The surgeon begins releasing the transversus abdominis muscle to access the preperitoneal plane, using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon creates a volcano sign in the surgical field and begins reducing the hernia contents, carefully manipulating the tissue to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon navigates the preperitoneal space lateral to the hernia defect, with visible anatomical landmarks such as the arcuate line and surrounding tissue structures.
The surgeon carefully manipulates fat tissue extending into the indirect inguinal ring, while visually identifying and preserving the cord structures to avoid injury.
The surgeon pulls tissue out of the hernia canal, reducing the hernia contents.
The surgeon creates space in the preperitoneal area to allow for wide mesh overlap, preparing the site for hernia repair.
The surgeon performs medial dissection in the myopectineal orifice, carefully navigating the area to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon repositions into the proper plane along the spermatic cord, preparing for further dissection or mesh placement.
The surgeon releases adipose tissue from the surface of the peritoneum, carefully separating the fat to expose the underlying structure.
The surgeon targets the myopectineal orifice (MPO) in the recurrent indirect hernia, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens within the surgical field, visible as a tubular structure near the dissected area.
The surgeon amputates tissue at the level of the internal ring, focusing on precise dissection within the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the cord structures and the vas deferens within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to parietalize the anatomical structures, separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies a lipoma tucked within the surgical field, likely near the previously mentioned structures.
The surgeon positions the mesh within the surgical field, ensuring it is adequately sized without using a ruler.
The surgeon proceeds to close the defect, likely using sutures or other closure techniques to secure the area after addressing the hernia.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh within the surgical space, preparing it for placement over the hernia defect.
The surgeon is satisfied with the mesh placement and proceeds to close the flap over the hernia defect.
